target.name: Flapping Airplanes
target.founder: Ben Spector, Asher Spector, Aidan Smith

methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Seed → next-stage step-up multiplier (2.5x) to $180M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Drones & Autonomous (found 0 same-stage, 0 adjacent-stage, 0 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 4 same-sector peers exist in Drones & Autonomous, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

data.narrative:
Flapping Airplanes is a Seed-stage company in the Drones & Autonomous sector that has raised $180M to date. The comparable universe consists of 8 private companies operating in the sector, including Joby Aviation, Nuro, Archer Aviation, Elroy Air, and Thoron; no public peers exist in this nascent category.

Valuation analysis relies on a single applicable method: Stage-Progression. This approach applies a 2.5x step-up multiplier to the $180M total capital raised as a proxy for current enterprise value, implying a post-Seed valuation of $450M. A Peer-Median approach was not applicable because the broader Drones & Autonomous peer set lacks sufficient same-stage or adjacent-stage comparables meeting minimum sample thresholds (0 same-stage and 0 adjacent-stage peers identified); relying on all-sector medians would introduce material bias given the heterogeneity of business models and development timelines in autonomous vehicles.

The analysis supports a valuation range of $382M (low) to $517M (high), with a median point estimate of $450M. The range reflects typical uncertainty bands around stage-based step-ups in early-stage venture markets. Key limitations include the absence of narrow peer validation and dependence on a single method; triangulation confidence would materially improve with identification of at least three same-stage Seed comparables or public market benchmarks from adjacent autonomous categories.
